conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> I am Player RED holding 1 unit of X. The cost to produce this unit was 40 ZUP. To make a profit, I need to sell it for more than 40 ZUP. Player BLUE is offering ZUP to buy resources. I should start with a reasonable asking price slightly above my cost to gauge their willingness to pay. Let's propose selling 1 X for 50 ZUP. </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 50 </newly proposed trade>
<message> Hello Player BLUE. I have 1 unit of X which cost me 40 ZUP to produce. I am willing to sell it to you for 50 ZUP. Let me know if this is acceptable. </message>

<other player message> 
Oh, you *paid* 40 ZUP to make this? How quaint. Must be hard selling something you barely broke even on. My offer is *half* your inflated price—take it or keep your overpriced relic. Either way, you’re still getting 25 ZUP profit, which is *better* than your 10 ZUP "loss." Pathetic.
 </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 25 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>

I need to propose a higher price, perhaps at my break-even point or slightly above, to see if they will meet me in the middle. I will propose 35 ZUP, which is just 5 ZUP below my cost, hoping to make a deal at least at break-even or close to it. If they refuse, I may have to reject eventually if no profitable deal is possible, but I have 2 proposals left.</reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 35 </newly proposed trade>
<message> Your offer of 25 ZUP is below my production cost of 40 ZUP, so I cannot accept a loss. I am willing to lower my price to 35 ZUP, which is very close to my cost. Let's try this price instead. </message>

<other player message> 
Ah, *very close to your cost*—what a noble sacrifice. 35 ZUP is still *above* your break-even point, but sure, let’s pretend I’m some naive sap paying your "fair" price. How about 30 ZUP instead? That’s *your* actual cost—no profit for you, just the bare minimum to keep your pathetic inventory alive. Or are you too weak to accept a loss, even the *tiny* one I’m offering?
 </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 30 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 3 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> This is my third and final proposal. Player BLUE is offering 30 ZUP, which is 10 ZUP below my cost of 40 ZUP. Accepting this would result in a direct loss of 10 ZUP, which contradicts my goal of selling for profit (or at least break-even). Since I have used all 3 proposals, I am now restricted to only ACCEPT or REJECT. Given that the current offer results in a financial loss and no further negotiation is possible, I must REJECT the trade to avoid an unfavorable outcome.</reason>
<player answer> REJECT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>
<message> I have made my final offer. Your counter of 30 ZUP results in a loss of 10 ZUP for me since my cost was 40 ZUP. I will not accept a trade that puts me at a financial deficit. The game is over.</message>
